title: What to Do the Summer Before College That Actually Matters
author: Tklesson Team
content:
You have graduated high school. The college acceptance letter is hanging on the refrigerator. The summer stretches out before you, and it feels like a waiting room. You know you should be doing something to prepare, but the standard advice is vague: read a lot, learn to do laundry, say goodbye to your friends. This advice is not wrong, but it is incomplete. The summer before college is a unique window of time with very few demands. Used well, it can make the difference between a rough first semester and a smooth one. Here is what actually matters.

Build the Life Skills You Will Need Immediately

College is not just an academic transition. It is a life transition. For many students, it is the first time they are fully responsible for feeding themselves, managing their own schedule, and solving their own problems. The students who struggle most in their first semester are often not the ones who are academically underprepared. They are the ones who cannot manage their time, their sleep, or their basic needs.

Learn to cook three simple meals. Not gourmet meals. Meals you can make in a dorm kitchen or a small apartment with minimal equipment. Pasta with vegetables. Eggs and toast. A stir-fry with rice. The student who can feed themselves cheaply and reasonably healthily has a significant advantage over the student who lives on dining hall pizza and vending machine snacks. Nutrition affects focus, mood, and energy. The dining hall will not always be open when you are hungry.

Learn to manage your own schedule. If your parents have been your alarm clock and your calendar, spend the summer practicing independence. Set your own wake-up time. Schedule your own appointments. Keep track of your own commitments without reminders from anyone else. The first few weeks of college will throw dozens of new demands at you. The student who arrives already practiced at self-management will handle the chaos better than the student who has never managed their own time.

Learn to do your own laundry. This is a cliché for a reason. Many students arrive at college having never operated a washing machine. The laundry room is not the place to learn, especially at midnight when you have run out of clean clothes. Learn now, when your parents are still nearby to answer questions.

Strengthen Your Academic Foundations

You do not need to pre-study your fall courses. The professor will teach the material when you arrive. What you should do is shore up any weak foundations that might make those courses harder than they need to be. If you are taking a math course in the fall and your algebra skills are rusty, spend a few hours a week reviewing. If you are a slow reader and your course load will be reading-heavy, practice reading for sustained periods. The goal is not to get ahead. It is to ensure you are not starting from behind.

Read for pleasure. This may be the last summer when you have significant unstructured time to read whatever you want. The reading load in college will be heavy, and most of it will be assigned. The student who arrives with a reading habit is better prepared for that load than the student who has not read a book for fun in years. Read fiction, nonfiction, graphic novels, whatever keeps you turning pages. The content matters less than the habit.

If you know your intended major, read something in that field that is not a textbook. A popular science book for a future biology major. A history of a period you find interesting for a future history major. The reading signals to your brain that this subject is not just a requirement. It is something you are genuinely curious about.

Invest in Your Relationships

Spend intentional time with the people who matter to you. Your family. Your closest friends. The relationships will not end when you leave for college, but they will change. The daily proximity that sustained them will be replaced by scheduled calls and occasional visits. The summer is a chance to bank connection before the distance arrives.

Have the conversations you have been avoiding. If there is tension with a parent or a sibling, address it. The unresolved conflict will not disappear when you move out. It will fester over phone calls and holiday breaks. A difficult conversation now is easier than months of avoidance.

If you are in a romantic relationship, talk honestly about what college means for it. Some high school relationships survive the transition. Most do not. Neither outcome is a failure, but both are easier when the expectations are clear. The conversation is uncomfortable. Avoiding it is worse.

Prepare Emotionally for the Transition

The first few weeks of college are disorienting for almost everyone. You will be lonely. You will miss home. You will wonder if you made a mistake. This is normal. Knowing it is normal does not make it easy, but it makes it survivable. The students who struggle most are often the ones who expected the transition to be effortless and interpreted their difficulty as a sign that something was wrong.

Have a plan for staying connected to your support system. Schedule a weekly call with your parents or a friend. The schedule is not a sign of weakness. It is a recognition that maintaining relationships across distance requires intention. The call gives you something to look forward to during the hard first weeks.

Identify what helps you manage stress, and practice it now. Exercise, journaling, meditation, music, whatever works. The coping strategies you develop during a low-stress summer are available to you during a high-stress semester. The student who arrives at college knowing how to calm themselves down has an advantage over the student who has never needed to.

The Bottom Line

The summer before college is not just a waiting period. It is a preparation window. Build the life skills you will need: cooking, scheduling, laundry. Shore up weak academic foundations without pre-studying. Invest in relationships with the people you are leaving. Prepare emotionally by normalizing the difficulty of the transition and developing coping strategies. The goal is not to arrive at college perfectly prepared. It is to arrive with enough of a foundation that the inevitable challenges are manageable.
